sample_information.sample_description_or_techmet_part_numbe: Three groups of hardmetal samples: (1) customer-supplied rods, (2) TechMet legacy inserts from 2019 lot, and (3) experimental wear plate coupons from internal trial PCS-WP-2409, all to be characterized for composition and hardness, with particular focus on cobalt content and microstructure for correlation to premature field wear.
sample_information.grade: Mixed – see individual line items for details. Primary interest is in confirming whether Sample 1 is comparable to standard C2/K20 and whether Sample 2 aligns with historical TM-15C documentation. Sample 3 is an in-house experimental blend without a formal commercial grade yet.

sample_notes_and_comments.sample_notes_and_comments:
These six rods were supplied by our end customer (Carolina Tool & Die) as part of a failure investigation on small end mills used in 4140 pre-hard (28–32 HRC) machining. The customer believes their previous supplier substituted a lower cobalt content material, leading to chipping along the cutting edges after relatively few cycles.

Each rod is approximately 10 mm diameter x 150 mm long. Two of the rods show visible discoloration and light surface oxidation near one end where they were clamped during a previous brazing trial; the other four rods appear visually clean. We engraved a small dot on one end of each rod with a fiber laser to distinguish them from our own stock, but this engraving is very shallow and should not affect test results.

Requested testing for Sample 1:
- Chemical composition (focus on WC, Co, and any grain growth inhibitors such as Cr, V, Ta, Nb).
- Hardness (HRA or HV30) on at least two rods to check for consistency across the lot.
- Basic microstructural evaluation if possible (grain size range, porosity, eta-phase presence) to compare against TechMet’s typical C2/K20 microstructure.

Context: We have compared edge wear on tools made from these rods vs. known TechMet C2 rods in our shop. Tools made from this customer material show flank wear roughly 30–40% faster based on a small sample run (10 pcs each), with more micro-chipping along the cutting edge. We are trying to determine if the material is significantly softer, has lower cobalt content, or has any abnormal microstructural features (e.g., coarse grain, eta-phase) that would explain the performance difference.

Please feel free to section or fully destroy at least three of the rods if needed for metallographic prep. We do not need any of these rods returned. If any rod must remain intact for your process documentation, that is fine, but it is not a requirement from our side.

These four brazed inserts are customer returns from a production batch shipped in late 2019 under TechMet grade TM-15C. They were originally used in a high-speed finishing operation on cast aluminum (A356) at approximately 18,000 rpm, low radial engagement. The customer recently reported an unusual pattern of edge chipping and small chunks missing from the cutting corner after relatively low cutting time.

Each insert is still attached to a steel tool body via brazing alloy. The inserts were removed from service after failure but not further modified. There is visible localized overheating near the braze joint on at least two tools (slight bluing of the steel, and some discoloration at the carbide/braze interface). We suspect either a brazing process issue (overheating or improper flux/braze selection) or a possible deviation in carbide grade from the original TM-15C spec.

Requested testing for Sample 2:
- Verify carbide grade versus historical TM-15C reference (chemical composition and hardness check).
- Examine the braze interface and adjacent carbide region for signs of overheating, microcracking, or braze alloy penetration into the carbide.
- If feasible, perform microhardness traverses from the cutting edge back toward the braze interface to see if there is any gradient or softening near the joint.

Context: Our internal records show TM-15C as a fine-grain, higher cobalt grade optimized for aluminum finishing. The customer reports that tools from earlier lots (2017–2018) performed very well, but this particular 2019 lot has a higher rate of corner chipping. We are trying to determine whether the failure is due to the carbide itself, the brazing process at our partner shop, or a combination of both.

We would like at least two of these inserts sectioned through the braze joint for metallographic examination. If possible, please document any observed microcracks or unusual phases at the interface. We do not need these particular assemblies returned, but we would appreciate a short note or images in the report that we can share with the customer’s quality department.

These three rectangular wear plate coupons are from our internal experimental batch PCS-WP-2409. The material is a proprietary trial blend we are exploring for abrasive wear resistance in a brick manufacturing application (high-silica clay with entrained sand). The coupons are approximately 20 mm x 30 mm x 5 mm, with both large faces ground to a uniform surface finish.

We cast and sintered these plates in-house using a modified powder blend that increases the carbide volume fraction and slightly reduces the cobalt binder content compared to our standard wear plate grade. We have not yet finalized the exact composition, but we have batch logs indicating the target Co content and additives used.

Requested testing for Sample 3:
- Hardness (preferably HV30, multiple indents per coupon to check uniformity across the surface).
- Microstructural evaluation (grain size distribution, binder distribution, porosity, any eta-phase or secondary carbides).
- Qualitative comparison to a typical 10–12% Co wear-grade microstructure if possible.

Context: In field trials, the test coupons showed promising wear performance (approximately 20–25% improvement over our current production grade, based on mass loss measurements after a standardized 72-hour run in the customer’s line). However, one coupon exhibited edge chipping when bolted into place with a slightly misaligned backing plate. We want to confirm that the microstructure is not excessively brittle and that there is no abnormal porosity or large carbide agglomeration that could lead to edge failures.

Please feel free to cut and mount these coupons as needed. We only need one intact sample for our own reference, but if destructive prep is required on all three to obtain a clear understanding of the structure and hardness variation, that is acceptable. We do not require any of these coupons to be returned.
